content:
Codebase

11 min read
Just now

--

Disclosure: I use GPT search to collection facts. The entire article is drafted by me.
You’ve just inherited a legacy embedded project. Three hundred thousand lines of C, forty-something directories, an RTOS buried somewhere, a driver layer nobody documented, and a protocol stack that “just works, don’t touch it.”
Your first instinct is to open main.c and start reading top to bottom. Two hours later, you've traced a function into the HAL layer, then into register writes, and you still cannot answer the one question that actually matters: what does this product do?
This is the single biggest mistake engineers make when facing an unfamiliar embedded codebase — treating code reading as a line-by-line comprehension exercise instead of a systems investigation. Reading a large embedded system is closer to reverse-engineering a machine than to reading a book. You don’t need to understand every gear. You need a model of how the whole thing moves.
1. What this actually is
“Reading an embedded codebase” sounds like a comprehension task, but it’s really three separate investigations stacked on top of each other: understanding what problem the system solves, understanding how modules are wired together, and understanding how data moves through those modules over time.
